coredata.dc:title: Sulfur-nitrogen doped carbon dots for Bi³⁺ ion detection and temperature sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Zhanbiao
Wang, Zhen
Ga, Lu
Ai, Jun
Liu, Pingping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, sulfur-nitrogen doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal approach using 2-Mercaptopyrimidine and citric acid as precursors. The as-prepared N,S-CDs exhibited a remarkable fluorescence response towards Bi³⁺ ions, achieving highly sensitive detection in the concentration range of 0–45 μM with a detection limit of 230 nM. These findings suggest great potential for the application of N,S-CDs in the detection of real water samples. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs demonstrated an excellent temperature response, showing reversible and recoverable fluorescence characteristics within the temperature range of 25 to 65 °C. The low cytotoxicity and in vitro cellular imaging properties displayed by this carbon dot further contribute to its enhanced functionality and practicality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bi3+detection
Fluorescence sensing
Temperature sensing
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of Bi3+ion detection
Calculation of detection limit (LOD) and quenching constant (Ksv)
Detection of Bi3+ based on N,S-CDs
Selectivity and interference of Bi3+ detection
Detection of Bi3+ in real samples
Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
Temperature sensing performance study of N,S-CDs
Cytotoxicity assays and bioimaging applications of N,S-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21864020, 82160652), the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (Grant No and 2019MS02014, 2018MS02012), \u2018\u2018Young Science and Technology Talents Program\u2019\u2019 (Leading Person) in Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Colleges and Universities (Grant No NJYT-19-A04), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Inner Mongolia Normal University, China, (Grant No 2022JBZD013). Funded by the research project of inner Mongolia Medical University Affiliated Hospital, Funded by Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Clinical Medicine Research Center of Nervous System Diseases, Funded by Hohhot Region High-quality Development and Advantageous Key Clinical Project of Neurological System Disease (No. 2023NYFY LHZD009).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.21864020, 82160652), the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (Grant No. and 2019MS02014, 2018MS02012), \u2018\u2018Young Science and Technology Talents Program\u2019\u2019 (Leading Person) in Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Colleges and Universities (Grant No. NJYT-19-A04), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Inner Mongolia Normal University, China, (Grant No. 2022JBZD013).Funded by the research project of inner Mongolia Medical University Affiliated Hospital, Funded by Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Clinical Medicine Research Center of Nervous System Diseases, Funded by Hohhot Region High-quality Development and Advantageous Key Clinical Project of Neurological System Disease (No. 2023NYFY LHZD009).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N,S-CDs; (b) Histogram of particle size distribution of N,S-CDs.
N,S-CDs(a) XPS full spectrum; (b) C1s; (c) N1s; (d) S2p.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs at Bi3+ concentrations of 0–45 μM; (b) Standard working curve at Bi3+ concentrations of 0–45 μM.

(a) compares the responses of N,S-CDs to various ions; (b) Compares the responses of N,S-CDs to various small molecules; (c) Compares the FL intensity of N, S-CDs after adding Bi3+and various other metal ions; (d) Compares the FL intensity of N, S-CDs after adding Bi3+and various small molecules.
(a) Comparison of fluorescence lifetimes; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs with different concentrations of Bi3+ ions added.
Emission spectra of N,S-CDs at different temperatures (a); Linear curve (b); Loop curve (c).
Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells cultured with N, S-CDs; (b) Bright field; (c) Overlay (λex = 405 nm).
Comparison of the prepared carbon points with other reported methods for Bi3+ determination.
N, S-CDs detected concentration of spiked Bi3+in water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Sulfur-nitrogen doped carbon dots for Bi³⁺ ion detection and temperature sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Enviromental Science
Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Chemistry and Enviromental Science, Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry, Inner Mongolia Normal University, 81 zhaowudalu, Hohhot 010022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, sulfur-nitrogen doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal approach using 2-Mercaptopyrimidine and citric acid as precursors. The as-prepared N,S-CDs exhibited a remarkable fluorescence response towards Bi³⁺ ions, achieving highly sensitive detection in the concentration range of 0–45 μM with a detection limit of 230 nM. These findings suggest great potential for the application of N,S-CDs in the detection of real water samples. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs demonstrated an excellent temperature response, showing reversible and recoverable fluorescence characteristics within the temperature range of 25 to 65 °C. The low cytotoxicity and in vitro cellular imaging properties displayed by this carbon dot further contribute to its enhanced functionality and practicality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, most of the research on Bi3+ ions have been focused on the synthesis of phosphors [1], chromides [2], and catalysts [3]. Bi3+ ions in the form of bismuth chloride are closely related to our life and are widely present in many fields, such as cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, batteries, etc., which indirectly leads to the fact that more and more Bi3+ ions begin to pose hazards to the environment and human body [4]. Although not as toxic as other ions (Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, or Cr3+), as one of the heavy metal ions, high concentrations of Bi3+ can also be extremely harmful to the human body. Therefore, it is important to explore simpler methods for detecting Bi3+. Electrochemical methods, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and atomic emission spectrometry are currently used to detect this ion [5,6]. However, these methods are not only demanding in terms of instrumentation, environment, and operation, but also have shortcomings such as being time-consuming and expensive. However, fluorescence-based detection methods can relatively compensate for these shortcomings.

Carbon dots (CDs) have many applications in optics, energy, and biomedicine due to their unique optical properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [7]. As a small-sized (usually <10 nm) carbon-based nanomaterial, CDs are expected to replace other conventional fluorescent nanomaterials in the development of high-performance probes. Not only because of their low price, ready availability, and excellent optical properties, but also because of the simplicity of the synthesis process and the possibility of doping and functionalisation for a wider range of applications [8]. In 2006, Sun and colleagues synthesized a carbon-based nanomaterial, named carbon dots, by laser ablation, but achieved only 10 % surface passivation [9]. They suggested that doping heteroatoms has proven to be an effective way to modulate the fluorescence and other properties of CDs. In addition to metal atoms, many non-metal atoms are involved in doping CDs to improve their properties. However, given that doping with metal atoms makes CDs more toxic and potentially environmentally unfriendly, many have chosen to dope CDs with non-metal atoms [10]. The most commonly used doping atoms are P, N, S, B, etc. For example, CDs modified with B and N are used to detect p-nitrophenol [11] or catechol [12]. CDs modified with S and N can detect cysteine [13], tetracycline [14], trimethylamine [15], baicalein [16], etc., in addition to various ions (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+) [17,18].

We found that there are very few carbon sites that are directly useful for the detection of Bi3+ ions compared to other ions. Therefore, we prepared N,S-CDs using C4H4N2S and C6H8N7•H2O (Scheme 1 ). Among different ions, N,S-CDs showed a high specificity for Bi3+ ions. The F0/F value showed a regular pattern concerning the ion concentration in the range of Bi3+ ion concentration from 0 to 45 μM (y = 0.0163x + 0.8583, R2 = 0.990). The experimental results showed that this fluorescent sensor has higher stability and salt resistance compared with previous fluorescent sensors. Sensor, this fluorescence sensor has higher stability and salt tolerance compared with previous fluorescence sensors. The sensitivity is also very high when detecting the presence of Bi3+ ions in real water samples. Furthermore, good temperature sensing properties were also demonstrated, and cell imaging was successfully performed. Thus, the prepared N,S-CDs are a promising multifunctional nanomaterial.

The water used during the experiment was ultrapure water (18.2 MQ•cm) prepared by Milli.Q ultrapure water system. The main reagents for the reaction are 2-Mercaptopyrimidine (C4H4N2S) (Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) and citric acid (C6H8N7•H2O) (Beijing Chemical Plant). All other reagents are supplied as Analytical Grade and do not need to be purified or reconstituted.

Transmission electron micrographs (TEM), UV–visible absorption spectra, and fluorescence spectra of this carbon spot were obtained on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), a U-2900 UV-visible absorption spectrometer (Hitachi High-Technologies, Inc.), and a F97pro-type fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai, China), respectively. The instruments used for the preparation were a D1008 centrifuge (Long Laboratory Instruments Co., Ltd. and a DL-108D intelligent ultrasonic cleaner (Shanghai Zhisheng Equipment Co., Ltd.). Blast drying oven (DHG-9015A, Shanghai-Scientific Instruments Co.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of Bi3+ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: To test whether N,S-CDs can be applied in real samples, the selectivity and anti-interference of Bi3+ ions are necessary. Therefore, the addition of various metal ions (Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K +, Fe3+, Cr3+, Bi3+) and biosmall molecules (dopamine, glycine, glutamic acid, citric acid, cysteine, glucose, glutathione Ascorbic acid) (all at a concentration of 0.75 mM) and fluorescence was measured to examine the selectivity for Bi3+ ions.

In anti-interference experiments, the above cations and small molecules were mixed into a solution of N,S-CDs containing Bi3+ ions as interfering substances, and then their fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of detection limit (LOD) and quenching constant (Ksv)
ce:section.ce:para: The calculations are described in the Supporting Information. The detection limit and Ksv of Bi3+were obtained to be 230 nM and 0.0163 μM-1, respectively.

The actual water sample was laboratory tap water, which was filtered through a disposable microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to remove particulate matter and insoluble matter from the water sample. A spiked sample was made by adding a standard solution of Bi3+ to this water sample and then measuring the level of Bi3+.

The morphology and microstructure of N,S-CDs prepared using C4H4N2S and C6H8N7•H2O are shown in Fig. 1 a, and the prepared N,S-CDs were uniformly distributed without obvious aggregation and with a mean size of about 4.15 nm (Fig. 1b). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) results showed a peak near 19.0°, which indicates that the N,S-CDs are essentially amorphous particles of carbon(Fig. S1a).

The peaks of OH/NH, CH, C = O, CN, and C-S bonds in the FTIR spectrograms of N,S-CDs (Fig.S1b) are located at 3206 [19], 2950 [20], 1711 [21], 1348, and 1180 cm-1, respectively [22,23]. The presence of these groups gives good hydrophilicity to the prepared N,S-CDs.

The major elements of N,S-CDs are carbon (81 %), oxygen (12.7 %), nitrogen (3.6 %), and sulphur (2.7 %) (Fig. 2 a). High-resolution XPS can show the combination pattern of these four elements. The three peaks of C1s are C -C /C = C (284.8 eV), C-S (285.9 eV), and COOH (288.9 eV), respectively (Fig. 2b) [24]. The peaks at 532.2 eV and 533.4 eV in the spectra of O1s confirm the presence of CO and C = O (Fig. S2) [25]. The 400.1 eV in the N1s spectrum corresponds to CN and 401.9 eV to NH(Fig. 2c). The presence of both peaks at 163.7 eV and 165 eV in the S2p spectrum suggests that sulphur is present in two forms (Fig. 2d).

As can be observed in Fig. S3a, the two distinct absorption peaks at 275 nm and 340 nm belong to the π-π* jumps of the C = C bond and the n-π* jumps of the C = O bond, respectively [26,27]. N, S CD with optimal λex = 397 nm and λem = 457 nm (Fig. S3b). Changes in the excitation wavelength of N,S-CDs caused changes in the position and intensity of their emission wavelengths (Fig. S3c,d), which are surface defects due to the uneven distribution of different functional groups on the surface.

The FL intensity of the prepared N,S-CDs remained unchanged after nearly eight months of storage in the refrigerator at 4 °C (Fig. S4a). The FL intensity did not fluctuate much between 0.5 and 4.5 M NaCl, which indicated that the N,S-CDs were stable in high salt concentration (Fig. S4b). In determining the optimal reaction time, a mixture of N,S-CDs, and Bi3+ was allowed to react in the dark, and the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs did not change drastically after 30 min, indicating that the optimal reaction time is 30 min (Fig. S4c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Bi3+ based on N,S-CDs
Selectivity and interference of Bi3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further test the specificity of Bi3+, we evaluated the effects of eight common small molecules and 15 cations on N,S-CD luminescence. Only Bi3+ was found to have a significant quenching effect on N,S-CD fluorescence (Fig. 4 a,b). Even when co-existing with other ions, the specificity of Bi3+ ions in quenching N,S-CDs is not affected (Fig. 4c,d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Bi3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: N,S-CDs are prepared so that they can be used in practical and complex environments. Therefore, it is an essential test whether N,S-CDs can detect Bi3+ ions in real samples. The results of detecting Bi3+ ions in tap water are shown in Table 2 , and the spiked recoveries of the prepared N,S-CDs ranged from 96.40 % to 101.56 %, with the RSDs all <3 % (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: We used fluorescence lifetimes and UV–vis spectroscopy to demonstrate the photoluminescence quenching mechanism of Bi3+. The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs was 4.17 ns in the absence of Bi3+ and decreased to 3.99 ns when Bi3+ was introduced, which could be attributed to the disruption of functional groups on the surface of N,S-CDs, suggesting that a dynamic quenching process occurred (Fig. 5 a). From Fig. 5b, it can be seen that the fluorescence signals of carbon dots are quenched to different degrees when different concentrations of Bi3+ are added. That is, the degree of quenching is related to the amount of Bi3+ added. Therefore, the reason for the quenching is the complexation of the surface functionality groups of N,S-CDs with Bi3+, which causes the fluorescence emission of N,S-CDs to be quenched, thus realizing the high-level quantitative detection of Bi3+ ions. The characteristic peak of Bi3+ absorption appeared at 275 nm, with no spectral overlap with the excitation and emission curves of N,S-CDs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching was not caused by energy transfer and internal filter effects, further confirming that dynamic quenching occurred [34]. As shown in Fig. S5, the prepared N,S-CDs is negatively charged while Bi3+ is positively charged, capturing electrons and undergoing electron transfer [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature sensing performance study of N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature-dependent emission spectra of aqueous phase N,S-CDs were recorded and analysed to study the effect of temperature on the optical properties of N,S-CDs. When the temperature of N,S-CDs is gradually increased from 25 °C to 65 °C, their emission intensity decreases accordingly (Fig. 6 a). The linear relationship between F/F0 and temperature (T) is shown in Fig. 6b: y = −0.0089x + 1.2121, R2 = 0.9954 (F0 and F refer to the FL intensity at 25 °C and above 25 °C). Such a phenomenon occurs because at low temperatures, the non-radiating channels are in an inactivated state. However, if the temperature is raised, the non-radiating channels are thermally activated, and this in turn leads to a reduction in emission [36]. N,S-CDs were submitted to three heating and cooling cycles (25–65 °C) to assess the stability of the temperature response of N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6c, the FL intensity of N,S-CDs can still be maintained at 97.4 % after three heating-cooling cycles, indicating that the prepared N,S-CDs are very reversible and reusable, and have a great potential for application in temperature sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assays and bioimaging applications of N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared carbon dots were small in size and had good stability properties. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs in HeLa cells was tested using the CCK-8 method. The viability of the cells was still higher than 98.68 % when N,S-CDs were persistently coexisting with the cells for 24 h at concentrations ranging from 0–200 µg/ml (Fig. S6). This suggests that further cell imaging experiments could be carried out to assess its feasibility in biological applications. As can be seen in Fig. 7 , cells co-incubated with N,S-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm exhibited intense bright blue fluorescence, indicating that N,S-CDs entered the cells smoothly and efficiently via endocytosis. In addition, there was no significant change in the morphology of the cells in comparison with the bright-field images in Fig. 7b These results suggest that N,S-CDs is a carbon spot with low cytotoxicity and good prospects for biological applications.

In summary, in this study, N,S-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using 2-mercaptopyrimidine and citric acid as precursors. The prepared N,S-CDs showed maximum fluorescence in the blue region near 457 nm wavelength, and the TEM image showed that the N,S-CDs were uniformly distributed, with a small-sized particle size, and that the N,S-CDs were highly stable. The N,S-CDs fluorescent probe showed significant fluorescence response to Bi3+ and achieved high sensitivity in the concentration range of 0–45 μM with a detection limit of 230 nM, and the results of this fluorescent probe in real water samples were satisfactory. In addition, the N,S-CDs exhibited excellent temperature responsiveness, showing reversible and recoverable fluorescence properties in the temperature range of 25 to 65 °C. The low cytotoxicity and in vitro cellular imaging properties of this carbon point further enhanced its functionality and utility. These results indicate that this N,S-CDs fluorescent probe is a promising and versatile novel nanomaterial.
Ethics approval and consent to participate Not applicable.
All authors agree to publish.
1 College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry, Inner Mongolia Normal University, 81 Zhaowudalu, Hohhot 010,022, China.
2 College of Pharmacy, Inner Mongolia Medical University, Jinchuankaifaqu, Hohhot 010,110, China.

Zhanbiao He: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhen Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Lu Ga: Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jun Ai: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Pingping Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21864020, 82160652), the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (Grant No and 2019MS02014, 2018MS02012), ‘‘Young Science and Technology Talents Program’’ (Leading Person) in Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Colleges and Universities (Grant No NJYT-19-A04), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Inner Mongolia Normal University, China, (Grant No 2022JBZD013). Funded by the research project of inner Mongolia Medical University Affiliated Hospital, Funded by Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Clinical Medicine Research Center of Nervous System Diseases, Funded by Hohhot Region High-quality Development and Advantageous Key Clinical Project of Neurological System Disease (No. 2023NYFY LHZD009).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2025.100482.
